text: This is an interesting case which demonstrates the advantage to the use of the Olympus IV case system as well as Thunderbeat. This is a patient who had a large central liver mass requiring a complex laparoscopic liver resection.

The surgeon has entered the peritoneal cavity, providing a clear view of the large liver tumor in the center of the screen. To the left of the screen, near the gallbladder, is an extension of the tumor that remains viable following embolization.

The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device to mobilize the falciform ligament and separate the anterior surface of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall, assessing the feasibility of laparoscopic resection.
The surgeon begins mobilizing and transecting the liver using the Thunderbeat device, which effectively cuts through the healthy liver tissue while providing hemostasis.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative ultrasound to assess the extent of the liver lesion, ensuring precise visualization despite any bleeding in the field, facilitated by the Olympus four case system.

The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound to assess the tumor's extent and plans to partially move the gallbladder to facilitate complete tumor removal. The Thunderbeat device is employed to transect the liver tissue, ensuring effective hemostasis during the resection.
The surgeon partially closes the Thunderbeat device and activates it, meticulously resecting the liver parenchyma with minimal bleeding visible.
The Thunderbeat device is used as the primary tool for dissection and resection of the liver, demonstrating its capability to provide excellent hemostasis during the procedure.

The surgeon connects the lines between the transection and resection margins in the liver. Blood is visible on the left side of the screen, but the optical system maintains clear visualization, allowing the surgeon to see droplets of blood and the unfolding of tissue planes during resection.

The surgeon achieves excellent hemostasis using the Thunder Beat device, despite minor nuisance bleeding. A branch of the middle hepatic vein is gently dissected, with visible thrombosed pores in the vein resulting from the device's action.

Blood is pooling in the surgical field, originating from the area to the left of the screen. The middle hepatic vein is visible with pore-like fenestrations, which are small branches that have been coagulated using the Thunder Beat device.

The surgical field appears red due to blood presence, yet anatomical details are clearly visible. A small hole in a branch of the middle hepatic vein is identified as the source of bleeding, with blood visibly trickling from it.
The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device in bipolar mode to seal a vein. The initial attempt is unsuccessful, but a second deployment effectively seals the vein, preventing further bleeding.

The surgical field displays excellent color contrast between yellow, brown, and red tissues. A small piece of cellulose film is placed to absorb excess blood, allowing the surgeon to continue with the dissection.
The surgeon isolates the inflow pedicle to segment four of the liver and creates a tunnel using blunt dissection. A surgical stapler is introduced to safely cut off the blood supply to this segment.

The surgeon fires the stapler across the inflow pedicle, revealing the resection plane with clear visualization despite minor oozing and bleeding. The liver's varied colors are visible, along with branches of blood vessels extending into the tumor, which the surgeon probes to assess the next steps for safe resection.
The surgical field appears relatively bloodless, revealing branches extending into the specimen that require ligation. The surgeon evaluates the best method for safely ligating these branches.

The surgeon places a clip on the staying side of a vessel branch to prevent bile leakage, then uses the Thunderbeat device to transect the specimen side, ensuring hemostasis during the hepatectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses the Thunderbeat device to dissect through the liver parenchyma, avoiding the staple line area where the device is ineffective. The section of liver near the staple line is left untouched for now, with plans to staple through it for safety.

The surgeon positions, closes, and fires the stapler, effectively taking most of the vasculature to the specimen. The Thunderbeat device is then used to unfold the remaining liver tissue layer by layer, continuing the resection process.

I believe that superior optics make operations like this possible and safer. Likewise, it is easier to teach trainees to identify correct anatomy when they can see it in clarity, even in a complex surgical field. We can see individual branches that can be clipped and then safely divided. This is obviously safe for patients.
The surgical field remains clear despite significant blood staining. The specimen is minimally attached, and the surgeon manipulates it to reveal the final attachments.

The surgeon completes the majority of the liver resection using the Thunderbeat device, supplemented by a few clip applications and two stapler firings to ensure hemostasis and secure tissue division.

The surgeon completes the hepatectomy, revealing the liver surface with small streams of blood indicating minor bleeding. The surgical field shows manageable blood loss, with some residual bleeding requiring attention.

The gallbladder specimen is moved aside following the completion of the cholecystectomy to assist with final hemostasis. Topical hemostatic agents are applied to the liver surface, and a sponge is placed over the agent with pressure applied for approximately two minutes to ensure effective hemostasis.

The surgical field is observed after the specimen has been extracted through a fan and steel incision, with no pneumoperitoneum present. The field appears dry, indicating successful hemostasis, and the patient's central venous pressure has been stabilized.
